community workgroup, organized Utah Department Health, been studying Medicaid expansion throughout summer.
cover more ground, workgroup split into five subgroups each focused specific approach: Full expansion; Partial expansion; Block grant; Full-family coverage; Charity care.

UHPP observed major developments during August meeting.
First, Sven Wilson, economist earned University Chicago, told workgroup that Medicaid expansion good, lean investment state.
Wilson also shared calculation that expanding Medicaid would cost Utah around $158 enrollee year (see page
Second, consensus building across multiple subgroups employ premium subsidies alternative traditional Medicaid expansion (see more below).
From UHPP's perspective, August meeting productive educational workgroup members general public.
However, idea receive enough attention: notion that federal government will only fund plan that covers Utah's entire Medicaid expansion population.
Most August meeting taken with presentations five subgroups.
Each subgroup least plan Block Grant group, headed Sen. Todd Weiler (R-Woods Cross) discussed three proposals.
Some presentations were substantive, others were more conceptual.
thing evident after hearing from groups: Progress being made Utah's policymakers lawmakers focus studying issue developing real solutions.
theme shared several subgroups premium subsidies enable low-income Utahns purchase private insurance instead Medicaid.
This approach would leverage federal funds help Medicaid beneficiaries purchase private coverage through state's health insurance exchanges.
Arkansas pioneered this approach, states like Ohio, Tennessee, Florida, Pennsylvania might follow lead.
Three subgroups (Full expansion, Partial expansion, Block Grant) considering subsidy-based alternatives Medicaid expansion.
good news that, done right, subsidy-based plan eligible federal match that starts 100% phases down 2020.
Unfortunately, UHPP's estimation, enough attention paid whether various premium-subsidy proposals would eligible 100% federal match.
Without full match, Utah would stuck with same finite resources current federal match rate.
Plus, federal government made clear that partial expansion plans won't receive full match rate.
Going forward, UHPP will work determine whether Utah's various premium-subsidy proposals will eligible full federal match.
